title: Distributed Cache — System Design
description: Design an in-memory caching layer (Memcached/Redis-style) that sits in front of a database to absorb read load at low latency. The themes are sharding the keyspace, eviction policy, the cache/DB consistency model, and surviving hot keys and node failures.

nodes.label: Application Service
nodes.description: Reads through the cache; falls back to the database on a miss.

nodes.label: Cache Client Library
nodes.description: In-app library that hashes keys to shards and talks to cache nodes.

nodes.label: Shard Router / Proxy
nodes.description: Optional proxy that maps keys to cache nodes via consistent hashing.

nodes.label: Cache Node A
nodes.description: Holds a partition of the keyspace in memory.

nodes.label: Cache Node B
nodes.description: Holds another partition of the keyspace.

nodes.label: Cache Node C
nodes.description: Holds another partition of the keyspace.

nodes.label: Cache Replica
nodes.description: Read replica / failover copy of a primary cache node.

nodes.label: Backing Database
nodes.description: Authoritative source of truth behind the cache.

nodes.label: Cluster Config / Membership
nodes.description: Tracks the ring topology and node health; clients refresh from it.

nodes.label: Eviction Manager
nodes.description: Per-node policy (LRU/LFU/TTL) reclaiming memory under pressure.

nodes.label: Metrics & Alerting
nodes.description: Collects hit rate, DB-fallback QPS, shard skew, evictions, replica lag, and epoch drift; drives alerts and backpressure.

functional: get(key) and set(key, value, ttl); delete(key).
Cache arbitrary serialized values keyed by string.
Scale capacity by adding cache nodes.
nonFunctional: Very low latency: sub-millisecond reads on a hit.
High hit rate: absorb the vast majority of DB read load.
Scale to ~1M+ ops/sec and terabytes of cached data.
Tolerate cache-node failure without taking down the application.
Bounded staleness: it's a cache, so some inconsistency with the DB is acceptable and must be reasoned about.

capacity.label: Reads per second
capacity.note: What the cache offloads from the DB.

capacity.label: Target hit rate
capacity.note: Misses fall through to the DB; the residual load must be survivable.

capacity.label: Value size
capacity.note: Larger values reduce entries per node.

capacity.label: Residual DB miss load
capacity.note: At 1M reads/s and 95% hit rate the DB still sees ~50k misses/s — before adding writes or stampede spikes — so 95% may be too low if the DB can't absorb that.

capacity.label: Node count (memory)
capacity.note: A few TB usable / ~64-128 GB RAM per node, derated ~30% for fragmentation/metadata, then ×2 for replication and ~25% headroom. Sizing is driven by value bytes + per-entry overhead, not key count.

capacity.label: Per-node QPS
capacity.note: 1M ops/s spread across ~20-40 nodes under uniform load — but provision extra headroom because hot keys and skew concentrate load on a few nodes.

capacity.label: Network bandwidth
capacity.note: 1M ops/s × ~KB values ≈ several GB/s aggregate across clients, cache nodes, replicas, and DB backfills — often the binding constraint before CPU.

capacity.label: Latency budget (p99)
capacity.note: A hit is same-AZ RTT + client serialization + cache CPU + connection-pool wait; the tail (p99) is dominated by queuing and GC, not the in-memory lookup.

api.description: Read a key; returns the cached value or a miss.
Write a value with an optional TTL.
Invalidate a key (e.g. after a DB write).

Batch/pipelined read of many keys in one round trip (the high-throughput read path). The client groups keys by owning node, issues one request per node in parallel, and returns hits plus the list of missing keys to load from the DB.
api.response: { user:42: value, user:43: value, user:99: MISS }

api.description: Production knobs layered on get/set/delete. namespace/key-prefix (e.g. "tenantA:user:42") scopes auth, quotas, metrics, and bulk invalidation per tenant. set(..., ttl, jitter) takes a relative TTL bounded by a max TTL with server-side jitter to de-synchronize expiries; reads do not refresh the TTL by default. set(..., cas=version) is a compare-and-set that only writes if the caller's version token still matches, so a stale read-miss backfill is rejected instead of clobbering a newer value. Value-size limits (e.g. reject > 1 MB, compress large values) keep one entry from skewing a shard.

fields.name: size_bytes
fields.type: int (entry footprint; drives sizing + value-size limits)

fields.name: expires_at
fields.type: timestamp (relative TTL + jitter, bounded by max TTL)

fields.name: version
fields.type: monotonic token for CAS / stale-backfill rejection

dataModel.name: ring / topology (config epoch)
dataModel.note: Control-plane mapping of key ranges to nodes; versioned by an epoch so clients can detect a stale ring.

fields.name: epoch
fields.type: monotonic version of the whole ring; clients fence on it

tradeoff: Memcached is a lean multithreaded slab cache (pure LRU, no persistence/replication) — simplest and fast for plain key-value; Redis/Valkey/Dragonfly add data structures, replication, persistence, and clustering at more operational surface.
Self-hosting gives full control over version, eviction tuning, and cost; a managed service trades that for patching, failover automation, and backups you don't run yourself.
makesIrrelevant: A managed cluster with built-in primary/replica failover removes much of the hand-rolled replication/promotion machinery in the failover step.
MemoryDB's durable, replicated log makes write-back-style durability a product feature rather than something you build.

tradeoff: A client library avoids an extra hop but puts ring/topology logic in every client and language; a proxy (mcrouter/Twemproxy/Envoy) keeps clients thin and centralizes pooling, coalescing, and metrics at the cost of a hop and an HA tier.
Cluster-native slot routing (Redis Cluster) bakes resharding/failover into the engine but constrains multi-key ops to the same slot.

azure.name: Managed by Azure Cache for Redis

tradeoff: A coordination service (ZooKeeper/etcd/Consul) gives you an authoritative, epoch-versioned ring and health source but is itself a service to run and keep available; gossip avoids a central dependency at the cost of weaker, eventually-consistent membership.
Managed caches hide this entirely — the trade-off becomes less control over failover timing and epoch semantics.
makesIrrelevant: A managed cache's built-in topology/health management removes the need to operate your own config/membership service.

tradeoff: Self-hosted Prometheus/Grafana gives high-cardinality per-key/per-shard metrics and custom alerting at the cost of running the stack; cloud monitoring is turnkey but coarser and metered.
Backpressure (DB-fallback budget, serve-stale, load shedding) is application/client logic regardless of which metrics backend you pick — the monitoring choice only feeds it signals.

patterns.name: Cache-aside (lazy loading)
patterns.what: App checks cache, loads from DB on miss, and backfills the cache.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy data tolerant of brief staleness; the default caching pattern.
patterns.group: Cache-Aside in Front of the Database

patterns.name: Consistent hashing
patterns.what: Map keys to cache nodes on a ring so adding/removing a node moves few keys.
patterns.whenToUse: Sharding a cache where node count changes and you want minimal cache churn.
patterns.group: Shard the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing

patterns.name: Eviction policy (LRU/LFU/TTL)
patterns.what: Reclaim memory by dropping least-useful entries when full.
patterns.whenToUse: Any bounded-memory cache; the policy shapes the hit rate.
patterns.group: Eviction: Make Room When Memory Is Full

patterns.name: Write-through / write-back
patterns.what: Update the cache on writes (synchronously or asynchronously) to control consistency.
patterns.whenToUse: When stale reads after a write are unacceptable, or to absorb write bursts.

patterns.name: Replication + failover
patterns.what: Keep a replica of each node so a failure doesn't drop a whole shard.
patterns.whenToUse: When a cold shard would overload the DB on failover.

patterns.name: Request coalescing (anti-stampede)
patterns.what: Collapse concurrent misses for the same key into one DB load.
patterns.whenToUse: Hot keys where a miss could trigger a thundering herd.

steps.title: 1. Naive: An In-Process Local Cache
description: Start with the simplest cache that helps: each application instance keeps a plain in-process map (a dict) of recently-read values. On a read, check the local map; on a miss, query the database and stash the result in the map. No extra infrastructure, microsecond hits.

It works for one process, but it doesn't solve the real problem. The cache isn't shared: every app instance has its own copy, so the same key is loaded and stored N times, and an invalidation on one instance leaves the others stale. The map is bounded by one process's heap, so it can't hold a large working set. And it starts cold on every deploy/restart, hammering the DB until it warms.
So the local map motivates a shared, dedicated cache tier: one logical cache all instances read through, sized far beyond a single heap, that survives app restarts.

view.caption: The application service reads and writes directly against the backing database, with no cache between them.

steps.decisionPrompt: You run 50 app instances, each with its own in-process map. How many times is one hot key loaded from the DB, and what happens when you deploy?
whyNow: Starting from a per-process dict makes the value of a shared cache tier obvious — every later step (sharding, eviction, consistency, failover) exists because the cache is shared and bounded, not local.

recap.after: Each app instance caches reads in its own in-process map.
recap.newRisk: The cache isn't shared (N cold copies, stale-on-invalidate), is capped by one process's heap, and starts cold on every restart.

traps.trap: Treating a per-instance in-process map as 'the cache'.
traps.why: It can't be shared, sized, or invalidated across instances, and dies on every restart — hit rate and consistency both suffer at scale.
traps.instead: Move to a shared cache tier all instances read through (cache-aside), then shard, evict, and replicate it.

steps.title: 2. Cache-Aside in Front of the Database
description: The application reads through the cache: on a hit it returns the value; on a miss it loads from the database and backfills the cache with a TTL.
This is the cache-aside (lazy-loading) pattern. The cache holds only what's actually been requested, and the DB stays the source of truth.
Even a single cache node here dramatically cuts DB read load — but one node caps memory and throughput and is a single point of failure.

view.caption: The application calls the cache client library to read node A; on a miss the application itself loads from the backing database and backfills the cache.

options.name: Cache-aside (default)
options.description: The application checks the cache, loads from the database on a miss, and writes the value back into the cache with a TTL.
pros: App controls the read path: on a miss it loads from the DB and backfills with a TTL, so the cache holds only what's actually requested.
Cache failure degrades gracefully — the app can still read straight from the DB and stay correct.
Simple to reason about; the DB remains the unambiguous source of truth.
cons: Every distinct key pays a cold-miss penalty the first time (and after eviction).
Read-miss backfill can race with a concurrent write and stash a stale value unless you delete-on-write.
Cache-population logic is duplicated in every caller unless wrapped in a shared client library.

view.caption: On a miss the application loads directly from the backing database and then backfills cache node A itself, so app code (not the cache) manages fills.

options.name: Read-through
options.description: Callers ask the cache node for a key and the cache node itself loads from the database on a miss and backfills.
pros: The cache node itself loads from the DB on a miss, so callers never touch the DB directly — one clean code path.
Load/backfill logic lives in one place (the cache tier), not scattered across every app instance.
Easier to add request coalescing centrally, since misses funnel through the cache.
cons: If the cache is down, reads fail unless you add an explicit DB fallback — less graceful than cache-aside.
The cache tier now needs DB credentials and a loader for every data type — more coupling.
Harder to use a managed key-value cache that doesn't support a read-through loader.

view.caption: The application reads through the client library to cache node A, which itself loads from the backing database on a miss.

steps.decisionPrompt: On a miss, who loads from the DB and backfills — the app or the cache node?

concepts.definition: App checks cache, reads DB on miss, then writes the value back into the cache.
concepts.whyItMatters: Simple, resilient (a cache outage just means more DB load), and caches only hot data.
concepts.example: GET user:42 -> miss -> SELECT from DB -> SET user:42 with TTL.
concepts.group: Cache-Aside in Front of the Database

whyNow: Cache-aside is the baseline contract; every later concern (sharding, eviction, consistency) is layered on this read path.

recap.after: Hot reads served from memory; DB load drops sharply.
recap.newRisk: One node limits memory/throughput and is a SPOF; need to shard.

traps.trap: Caching on write only (never on read).
traps.why: Cold or evicted keys never repopulate, so hit rate collapses for read-heavy data.
traps.instead: Lazy-load on miss so the working set self-populates.

flows.name: Cache-aside read with backfill
flows.note: The app asks the client lib for a key; on a hit the cache node returns it, on a miss the app itself loads from the DB and backfills the cache.

steps.title: 3. Shard the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing
description: To exceed one node's memory and throughput, partition keys across many cache nodes. Hash each key to a node on a consistent-hashing ring (with virtual nodes for even load).
Consistent hashing means adding or removing a node only remaps that node's share of keys — not the whole keyspace — so a scaling event doesn't blow away the entire cache and stampede the DB.
Routing can live in a client library (client hashes and talks to nodes directly) or a proxy/router tier; both read the ring topology from a config/membership service.

Topology-change rollout matters as much as the hash function. Adding or removing a node is staged: the config service publishes a new ring epoch, token ranges move gradually rather than all at once, and the remapped keys refill lazily on miss by default — with active warming reserved for known-hot ranges. Throttle DB fallback during a planned reshard so the unavoidable miss wave doesn't stampede the DB, and alert on clients lagging the current epoch (a straggler routes to the old owner).

Placement rule: assign a key's virtual nodes — and especially its primary and replica — to different fault domains (host, rack, AZ) so a single zone event can't remove a primary and its replica together.

view.caption: The client library routes each key through the shard router to cache node A, B, or C, with cluster config feeding ring and health.

options.name: Client-side hashing (default)
options.description: Each client embeds the ring and consistent-hashes a key locally, then talks straight to the owning cache node with no intermediary.
pros: No extra network hop: the client library hashes the key and talks to the owning cache node directly.
No proxy tier to provision, scale, or keep highly available.
Lowest possible latency for a hit — one round trip to the right node.
cons: Every client embeds ring/membership logic and must refresh topology consistently — fat clients.
A ring-config change must propagate to all clients; stragglers route to the wrong node.
Hard to support many languages — each needs a correct, in-sync client implementation.

view.caption: The client library reads the ring from cluster config and hashes each key directly to cache node A, B, or C with no proxy hop.

options.name: Proxy routing
options.description: A proxy tier owns the ring; clients hit one endpoint and the proxy hashes each key and forwards it to the owning node.
pros: Clients stay thin — they hit one endpoint and the proxy owns the ring and routing.
Topology changes are absorbed at the proxy; clients need no redeploy.
Centralizes cross-cutting features: connection pooling, request coalescing, metrics.
cons: Adds a network hop and a tier that must itself be scaled and made highly available.
The proxy can become a throughput bottleneck or a new single point of failure.
More operational surface than a pure client library.

view.caption: The client library sends keys to a shard router that routes them by range to cache node A, B, or C using ring and health from cluster config.

steps.decisionPrompt: With plain key % N hashing, what happens to the hit rate when one node dies?

concepts.definition: Hash keys and nodes onto a ring; a key belongs to the next node clockwise.
concepts.whyItMatters: A node change remaps ~1/N of keys instead of nearly all — avoids a cache-wide miss storm.
concepts.example: Drop one of 10 nodes -> only ~10% of keys remap, not 100%.
concepts.group: Shard the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing

whyNow: Sharding is how the cache scales; consistent hashing is specifically what protects the DB during topology changes.

recap.after: Keyspace spread across nodes; scaling moves minimal keys.
recap.newRisk: Each node has bounded RAM — it must decide what to drop when full.

traps.why: Changing node count remaps almost every key, causing a near-total cache miss and a DB stampede.

traps.trap: Flipping the whole ring to a new topology in one step.
traps.why: All remapped keys miss at once, so a routine scale-up turns into a DB stampede.
traps.instead: Publish a new epoch, move token ranges gradually, refill lazily (warm hot ranges), and throttle DB fallback during the reshard.

flows.name: Add a node (staged reshard)
flows.note: Scaling up is rolled out, not flipped: the new node joins, config publishes a new epoch, a slice of token ranges moves to it, clients refresh the ring, and the moved keys refill lazily on miss under a throttle so the DB isn't stampeded.

messages.label: assign vnodes in fresh fault domains; advance epoch

steps.title: 4. Eviction: Make Room When Memory Is Full
description: Cache memory is bounded, so when a node fills up it must evict entries. The policy determines which entries survive and therefore the hit rate.
LRU (least-recently-used) is the common default; LFU (least-frequently-used) better protects truly hot keys from a burst of one-offs; TTL expiry bounds staleness regardless.

Production caches often use scan-resistant variants — segmented LRU (SLRU) or TinyLFU (the admission policy in Caffeine) — that combine recency with a cheap frequency sketch so a one-time scan can't flush the hot working set, getting LFU's scan resistance at close to LRU's cost.
Eviction is per-node and continuous. Size values and TTLs so the working set fits; monitor evictions/sec as an early warning that the tier is undersized.

view.caption: The eviction manager evicts entries from cache node A under memory pressure; the next miss reloads via the application from the backing database and backfills.

options.name: LRU (default)
options.description: When memory fills, the node drops the entry whose last access is oldest, tracked cheaply via an intrusive recency list.
pros: Evicts the least-recently-used entry — a good general proxy for what won't be needed soon.
Cheap O(1) to maintain with an intrusive list, so it adds negligible per-op cost.
Predictable and well understood; the default in most cache servers.
cons: A large one-time scan of cold keys evicts genuinely hot entries (cache pollution).
Treats a key read once and a key read 10,000 times the same once both are 'recent'.
No frequency awareness, so bursty one-offs can flush the working set.

view.caption: The eviction manager drops the least-recently-used entries from cache node A under pressure; a later miss reloads via the application from the database.

options.name: LFU
options.description: Each entry carries an access-frequency counter and the node evicts the least-frequently-used entry when memory is full.
pros: Keeps truly hot keys resident even when a scan floods the cache with one-off reads.
Better hit rate for skewed access patterns where a few keys dominate traffic.
Resists the LRU scan-pollution problem directly.
cons: Needs per-entry frequency counters — more memory and maintenance overhead.
Cold-starts poorly: new-but-soon-popular keys are evicted before they build a count.
Stale frequency can pin keys that were hot yesterday but aren't today (needs aging).

view.caption: The eviction manager removes the least-frequently-used entries from cache node A; evicted keys reload via the application from the backing database on demand.

options.name: TTL-only
options.description: Entries simply expire at their TTL with no recency or frequency tracking; nothing else decides what to drop.
pros: Bounds staleness regardless of access pattern — every entry expires on a clock.
No recency/frequency bookkeeping; simplest possible policy to operate.
Pairs well with invalidate-on-write to cap how long a stale value can live.
cons: Doesn't protect memory under pressure: a flood of new keys can still OOM the node.
Synchronized expiries of a hot key cause a thundering herd at the TTL boundary.
Tuning a single TTL for mixed hot/cold keys is a blunt instrument.

view.caption: The eviction manager lets entries in cache node A expire by TTL rather than by access; the application refills from the database after they lapse.

steps.decisionPrompt: A scan reads a million cold keys once. Why might that hurt your hit rate under LRU, and what helps?

concepts.definition: Evict the least-recently-used vs least-frequently-used entry.
concepts.whyItMatters: LRU is cheap but vulnerable to scans; LFU protects hot keys but needs frequency tracking.
concepts.example: A one-time scan evicts hot keys under pure LRU; LFU (or scan-resistant LRU) keeps them.
concepts.group: Eviction: Make Room When Memory Is Full

whyNow: Eviction is what turns 'infinite cache' fantasy into a real bounded-memory tier; the policy choice directly drives the hit-rate requirement.

recap.after: Bounded memory with a policy that protects hot data.
recap.newRisk: After a write to the DB, cached copies are stale — need a consistency strategy.

steps.title: 5. Cache/DB Consistency on Writes
description: The cache and DB can disagree after a write. Pick a strategy: cache-aside with invalidation (delete the key on DB write, repopulate lazily), write-through (write cache + DB synchronously), or write-back (write cache now, flush to DB async).

Invalidate-on-write is the common, robust choice: it avoids serving the old value and lets the next read repopulate. Write-through keeps the cache warm but adds write latency; write-back is fast but risks loss on node failure.

Name the race: a read-miss can backfill a stale value if it interleaves with a write. Mitigate with delete (not update) on write, short TTLs, or a version token — backfill with compare-and-set so a fill carrying an older version than the current entry (or than the DB row it read) is rejected instead of clobbering fresh data.

view.caption: On a write the application updates the backing database and deletes (invalidates) the key from cache node A; the next read misses and reloads via the application.

options.name: Invalidate-on-write (delete key) (default)
options.description: On a database write the app deletes the cached key, so the next read misses and repopulates from the source of truth.
pros: Deleting the key on a DB write guarantees the next read repopulates from the source of truth — no stale serve.
Avoids the lost-update race of writing a computed value into the cache out of order.
Cheap: one delete, and only re-read keys pay the reload cost.
cons: The first read after every write is a guaranteed miss (briefly colder cache).
A read-miss backfill can still interleave with the write and re-stash a stale value — needs short TTLs or versioned keys.
Hot keys get re-loaded by many readers right after invalidation (pair with coalescing).

view.caption: On a write the application updates the backing database and deletes the key from cache node A; the next read misses and the application reloads it.

options.name: Write-through cache
options.description: Each write updates the cache and the database synchronously in the same request, so the cache always holds the fresh value.
pros: Cache and DB are updated together, so the cache stays warm and reads after a write hit immediately.
No post-write cold miss; good for read-heavy keys that are written occasionally.
Simple consistency story when the write succeeds to both stores.
cons: Adds cache write latency to every write, even for keys that are never read again.
A two-store write needs care if one side fails (partial update / rollback).
Wastes memory caching write-only keys that are never read back.

view.caption: The application writes synchronously to both cache node A and the backing database so the two stay in lockstep.

options.name: Write-back (write-behind)
options.description: Writes land in the cache and return immediately, and a background flush asynchronously persists them to the database later.
pros: Writes hit the cache and return immediately, then flush to the DB asynchronously — lowest write latency.
Coalesces bursts of writes to the same key into fewer DB writes.
Best fit for write-heavy, loss-tolerant workloads (e.g. counters, view tallies).
cons: A cache node failure before flush loses accepted writes — unacceptable for durable data.
The DB lags the cache, so other readers/services see stale data.
Needs durable buffering and replay machinery to be remotely safe.

view.caption: The application writes to cache node A, which asynchronously flushes the change to the backing database later.

steps.decisionPrompt: Update the DB then the cache, or delete the cache then update the DB? What can go wrong in each order?

concepts.definition: Delete the cached key when the DB changes, letting the next read repopulate.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids the hard problem of keeping two copies perfectly in sync.
concepts.example: UPDATE users SET ... -> DELETE cache user:42 -> next GET reloads fresh.

concepts.definition: Write cache+DB together vs write cache now and flush DB later.
concepts.whyItMatters: Trades write latency and durability against freshness.
concepts.example: Write-back absorbs bursts but can lose unflushed writes if the node dies.

whyNow: Consistency is the subtle part interviewers push on; articulating invalidate-on-write and the read-miss race is the senior differentiator.

flows.name: Invalidate-on-write
flows.note: On a write the app updates the DB, then deletes the cached key; the next read misses and reloads the fresh value from the source of truth.

flows.name: Stale backfill rejected by version (CAS)
flows.note: A read-miss reads version v6 from the DB, but a concurrent write advances the row to v7 and deletes the key before the slow reader backfills. The backfill is a compare-and-set tagged v6; the cache (now empty or holding v7) rejects it, so the stale value never lands.

recap.before: Reads could serve stale data indefinitely.
recap.after: Writes invalidate; staleness is bounded and reasoned about.
recap.newRisk: A single hot key can still overwhelm one node, and node failure drops a shard cold.

traps.trap: Updating the cache value on write instead of deleting it.
traps.why: Concurrent writes can interleave and leave the wrong value cached.
traps.instead: Delete (invalidate) and let the next read repopulate the authoritative value.

steps.title: 6. Hot Keys and Thundering Herds
description: A single very popular key (a celebrity profile, a flash-sale item) concentrates traffic on one node — a hot shard the ring can't spread.

When that key expires or is evicted, all concurrent readers miss at once and hammer the DB: a cache stampede / thundering herd. Coalesce concurrent misses so only one request loads from the DB and the rest wait for it.
For extreme hot keys, replicate the key across multiple nodes (or add a tiny local in-process cache) so reads spread; refresh popular keys slightly before expiry to avoid synchronized misses.

Make the control loop explicit: detect hot keys from per-key/per-shard request counters (a sampled top-K sketch on each node); promote a key to replicated status once it crosses a QPS threshold, sizing the replica count by how far it exceeds one node's budget; fan invalidation out to every replica on write; and demote (drop the extra copies) once the key cools below the threshold, so memory isn't wasted on yesterday's hot key.

view.caption: A hot key on cache node A is copied to a cache replica so reads spread; on a miss the application still loads from the backing database and backfills.

options.name: Request coalescing (single-flight) (default)
options.description: On a miss, the first request takes a per-key lock and loads from the database while concurrent readers wait for that one result.
pros: On a miss, only one request loads from the DB while the rest wait for the result — caps DB load at one query per key.
Directly kills the thundering herd when a hot key expires.
Cheap and local: a per-node in-flight map, no extra infrastructure.
cons: All concurrent readers of that key block on one in-flight load — a slow DB read stalls many.
Coordination is per-node, so the herd can still hit once per cache node holding the key.
Doesn't spread the read load of a sustained-hot key, only the miss storm.

view.caption: On a hot-key miss the client library lets a single in-flight request load from the backing database while concurrent readers wait for that one backfill.

options.name: Replicate the hot key across nodes
options.description: The hot key is copied onto several cache nodes so reads of it spread across shards instead of dogpiling one owner.
pros: Copies of the hot key live on multiple nodes, so reads spread instead of dogpiling one shard.
Scales read throughput for a sustained-hot key beyond a single node's capacity.
Removes the single hot shard the ring otherwise can't spread.
cons: Invalidation must fan out to every replica of the key — more write complexity.
Requires detecting which keys are hot and managing their replica set dynamically.
Wastes memory replicating keys that aren't actually hot if detection is coarse.

view.caption: The hot key is replicated from cache node A to a cache replica so reads spread across both copies; the application backfills from the database on a miss.

options.name: Tiny local in-process cache (L1) in front
options.description: Each app instance keeps a tiny short-TTL local cache that serves the few hottest keys before the shared tier is consulted.
pros: A small per-app-instance cache absorbs reads of a few very hot keys at microsecond latency.
Offloads the shared tier entirely for the hottest keys — no network hop, no single hot shard.
Trivial to add as a bounded LRU with a very short TTL.
cons: Reintroduces the staleness window of the naive local cache for those keys.
Memory duplicated across all N app instances; only worth it for a tiny hot set.
Invalidation can't reliably reach every instance — bound it with a short TTL.

view.caption: Each application instance checks a tiny local L1 cache first and only falls through to the shared tier, then the application loads from the database on a full miss.

steps.decisionPrompt: 10,000 requests/sec read one key. It expires. What happens to the DB, and how do you prevent it?

concepts.definition: Many concurrent misses for the same key flooding the backing store at once.
concepts.whyItMatters: A single expiry can spike DB load enough to cause an outage.
concepts.example: Hot key TTL fires -> 10K simultaneous misses -> DB overload.

concepts.definition: Letting one request fill the cache while concurrent misses wait for its result.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns a herd into a single DB load.
concepts.example: First miss takes a per-key lock and loads; others block briefly, then read the backfilled value.

whyNow: Hot keys are the failure mode sharding alone can't fix; coalescing + key replication is the expected mitigation.

recap.newRisk: A node failure still drops its whole shard cold — need replication/failover.

traps.trap: Relying on consistent hashing to spread a single hot key.
traps.why: One key hashes to exactly one node; the ring can't split it.
traps.instead: Replicate the hot key across nodes / add a local cache, and coalesce misses.

flows.name: Coalesced miss on a hot key
flows.note: When a hot key misses, only the first reader loads from the DB; concurrent readers wait and then read the backfilled value, turning a stampede into a single load.

steps.title: 7. Replication, Failover, and Failure Modes
description: If a cache node dies, its entire shard is suddenly cold and every key on it misses to the DB — potentially a load spike that takes the DB down. Mitigate by keeping a replica per primary that can take over warm.

Replication contract: because the DB is authoritative, cache replication can be asynchronous (replicas lag the primary by a bounded amount). Staleness is capped by TTL/version and the cache-aside miss fallback, so a slightly stale warm replica is still better than a cold shard. The client writes to the primary; the primary streams to its replica. Promotion is driven by the config/membership service, not by the client or replica unilaterally: a health check fails, the config service advances the ring epoch, and clients/routers refresh and reroute. Reads then go to the promoted replica (or, with virtual nodes, the failed node's keys spread across many peers rather than dogpiling one).

Guard against the dangerous middle state: a primary suspected dead but still serving. Fence with the config epoch so clients converge on one owner instead of split-brain routing, and prefer a brief miss-to-DB over reading two divergent copies.

Placement and warmup decide how cheap failover really is. A primary and its replica must sit in different fault domains (host, rack, AZ) so one zone event can't take both. After a promotion or a node add, admit traffic gradually and rate-limit rewarming rather than slamming the DB to refill a cold range all at once.
Treat the cache as best-effort: the app must remain correct (if slower) with the cache empty. Add jittered TTLs and rate-limited backfill so a cold start doesn't stampede the DB.

view.caption: Cache node A replicates asynchronously to a replica; when A fails, config advances the ring epoch, the client library refreshes and reroutes reads to the replica, and the application backfills from the database for anything still cold.

steps.decisionPrompt: A cache node holding 10% of keys dies. What does the DB suddenly experience, and how do you cushion it?
whyNow: Closing on failure modes shows the cache improves performance without becoming a new SPOF — the reliability requirement.

recap.before: Node loss = cold shard + DB spike.
recap.after: Replicas warm-failover; ring routes around failures; cold starts are cushioned.
recap.newRisk: Replication doubles memory cost; stale replicas — bounded by acceptable cache staleness.

flows.name: Primary failure -> epoch-driven failover
flows.note: A health check fails, the config service advances the ring epoch, clients refresh and reroute to the warm replica, and rate-limited backfill from the DB fills anything still cold.

messages.label: health check fails on primary; advance ring epoch

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Its replica serves the shard warm; the config epoch advances and clients route to it; the DB sees little extra load.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-primary async replica + health-driven epoch routing + virtual nodes.

failureDrills.scenario: The entire cache tier is unavailable.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The app still works (correctly) by reading the DB directly, just slower.
failureDrills.mitigation: Cache-aside keeps the DB authoritative; rate-limit/jitter backfill to avoid stampede on recovery.

failureDrills.scenario: Config-service staleness / split-brain routing (the subtle client-side-hashing failure).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Clients on an old ring epoch route to a removed or wrong owner; some keys are read/written against two different owners.
failureDrills.mitigation: Version the ring with a config epoch and fence on it; converge clients on one owner, prefer a brief miss-to-DB over serving two divergent copies, and alert on epoch drift across clients.

steps.title: 8. Operations, Backpressure, and Warmup
description: A distributed cache rarely fails by losing data — it fails by overload and feedback loops. A miss storm can take the DB down, a bad config epoch can split traffic, uncontrolled hot-key replication wastes memory, and a 'warm' failover becomes cold if backfill is unbounded. The interview-level skill is naming what the system does when a metric crosses a threshold, not just which metrics exist.

Backpressure actions (what the system does under stress): rate-limit DB backfill to a fallback budget, shed optional cache-fill work, serve stale for eligible keys when the DB is the bottleneck, jitter TTLs to de-synchronize expiries, cap per-key single-flight waiters so one slow load can't pile up unbounded, and throttle rewarming after a node add or failover.

Alerts named by symptom and owner: DB-fallback QPS over budget (cache team — risk of DB overload), per-shard QPS skew (hot shard), high evictions/sec or low memory headroom (undersized tier), replica lag (failover won't be warm), stale config epoch across clients (split-brain risk), and rising p99 cache-hit latency (queuing/GC, not the lookup). Each should map to one of the backpressure actions above.

The hardest case is the cold start: cache empty and the DB fallback budget already exhausted. Degrade deliberately rather than collapsing — admit refill traffic gradually, serve stale where allowed, and shed or queue non-critical reads — so the DB recovers instead of being held down by the very traffic trying to rewarm the cache.

view.caption: Cache nodes and the application emit signals (hit rate, evictions, DB-fallback QPS, replica lag, p99) to metrics & alerting, which feeds backpressure back to the client library — shed optional fills, serve stale, and throttle DB backfill before the DB is overloaded.

steps.decisionPrompt: Your DB-fallback budget is 60k QPS and a cold start is generating 200k misses/sec. What does the cache do so the DB recovers instead of staying down?

concepts.definition: A cap on how much miss/backfill traffic the cache is allowed to send the DB per second.
concepts.whyItMatters: It converts an unbounded miss storm into a known, survivable load — excess is shed, served stale, or queued.
concepts.example: Budget 60k QPS; a cold start wanting 200k is throttled to 60k and the rest serve stale or wait.

concepts.definition: Returning an expired-but-recent value when the backing store is overloaded, instead of forcing a miss.
concepts.whyItMatters: Trades a little extra staleness for keeping the DB alive during a spike — the cache stays useful when it's needed most.
concepts.example: DB-fallback over budget -> eligible keys serve their last value past TTL until load drops.

whyNow: Once the architecture is complete, what separates a senior answer is describing the operating model — backpressure, budgets, and warmup — that keeps the whole thing from taking down the DB it was meant to protect.

recap.before: The mechanisms exist but their failure-under-load behavior is implicit.
recap.after: Explicit backpressure actions, symptom-named alerts, and a deliberate cold-start degradation path.
recap.newRisk: Backpressure policy itself must be tuned and tested — wrong thresholds either overload the DB or shed traffic needlessly.

traps.trap: Treating the DB as infinitely able to absorb cache misses.
traps.why: A cold start or mass eviction sends a miss storm that exceeds DB capacity and causes an outage the cache was meant to prevent.
traps.instead: Set an explicit DB-fallback budget and rate-limit/serve-stale/shed once it's exceeded.

traps.trap: Rewarming a cold shard at full speed after a node add or failover.
traps.why: Uncontrolled refill of a whole range stampedes the DB exactly when it's already stressed.
traps.instead: Admit traffic gradually and throttle backfill so warming is paced, not instantaneous.

failureDrills.scenario: DB-fallback budget exhausted during a cache cold start.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The cache caps DB backfill at the budget; excess reads serve stale where eligible, are coalesced per key, or shed/queued. Hit rate climbs gradually as the working set rewarms; the DB stays within capacity instead of being driven into overload.
failureDrills.mitigation: Explicit DB-fallback budget + per-key single-flight + serve-stale eligibility + paced rewarming, with an alert when sustained miss demand exceeds the budget.

failureDrills.scenario: Per-shard QPS skew (one hot shard) while the rest are idle.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The skew alert fires; the hot key is promoted to replicated status (or pulled into the local L1) so its reads spread, and coalescing caps its DB load.
failureDrills.mitigation: Top-K hot-key detection -> replicate/L1 promotion -> demote when it cools.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Sharded, Replicated Cache Tier
finalDesign.description: Default design: applications read cache-aside through a client library that consistent-hashes keys directly across many in-memory cache nodes, with virtual nodes for even load and a config service tracking topology/health (epoch-versioned). On a miss the application loads from the database and backfills the cache; the database always remains the source of truth. Each node evicts under memory pressure (LRU/LFU + TTL with jitter). Writes invalidate cached keys to bound staleness, with an optional version/CAS to reject stale backfills. Hot keys are replicated and concurrent misses coalesced to prevent stampedes; per-primary async replicas in separate fault domains provide warm failover so a node loss doesn't cold-spike the database, with rate-limited backfill cushioning cold starts. Operationally, metrics drive backpressure: a DB-fallback budget, per-shard QPS and hot-key alerts, evictions/sec and memory headroom, replica lag, and config-epoch drift across clients, with serve-stale and paced rewarming under load. The proxy-routing variant (thin clients, central metrics/quotas, at the cost of a hop and another HA tier) and read-through/write-through (cache tier owns DB loading) are deliberate alternatives, shown as option tabs rather than the default.

options.name: Client-library routing (default)
options.description: Apps read cache-aside through a client library that consistent-hashes keys directly to the owning cache node — no proxy hop. This is the default design.

view.caption: Default path: the application calls the client library, which consistent-hashes keys directly to cache nodes A/B/C; on a miss the application loads from the database and backfills. Nodes replicate to a warm replica in another fault domain, the eviction manager reclaims memory, config tracks the epoch-versioned ring/health, and metrics drive backpressure. No proxy tier.

options.name: Proxy-routing variant
options.description: A proxy/router tier owns the ring; clients hit one endpoint and the proxy forwards each key to the owning node. Thin clients and centralized metrics/quotas, at the cost of an extra hop and another HA tier.

view.caption: Proxy variant: the client sends keys to a shard router that consistent-hashes and forwards them to cache node A/B/C; config feeds the router the epoch-versioned ring/health. The cache tier (replicas, eviction, metrics, DB fallback) is otherwise identical to the default.

Default path: the application calls the client library, which consistent-hashes keys directly to cache nodes A/B/C; on a miss the application loads from the database and backfills. Nodes replicate to a warm replica in another fault domain, the eviction manager reclaims memory, config tracks the epoch-versioned ring/health, and metrics drive backpressure. The proxy-routing variant is on the option tab above.

functional.how: Cache-aside read path; per-entry TTL; invalidate on write.

nonFunctional.how: In-memory nodes reached directly via client hashing.
Lazy load + eviction policy that protects hot keys.
Horizontal sharding across many nodes' RAM.
Replica failover + ring routing; DB stays authoritative.
DB-fallback budget with rate-limited backfill, serve-stale, single-flight, and paced rewarming so misses can't overload the DB.

say: Confirm it's a look-aside cache in front of a DB, read-heavy.
Pin sub-ms hits and a high hit rate as the goals.
Agree staleness is acceptable but must be bounded and reasoned about.
Shard with consistent hashing + virtual nodes.
Cache/DB consistency: invalidate-on-write and the read-miss race (version/CAS to reject stale backfills).
Hot keys and thundering herds: coalescing + key replication.
Replication/failover so node loss doesn't cold-spike the DB.
Operations: DB-fallback budget, backpressure (serve-stale, throttle), and paced warmup.
Tradeoffs: eviction policy, write-through vs invalidate, replication cost.
Extensions: multi-tier (local + remote) caching, near-cache, persistence.

expectations: May miss stampedes and consistency races.
Uses consistent hashing and reasons about eviction policy.
Reasons about cold-shard DB spikes on failover and cushions them.
Discusses write-through vs write-back tradeoffs, multi-tier caching, and consistency races precisely.
Defines an operating model: DB-fallback budget, backpressure actions, symptom-named alerts, replica placement across fault domains, and staged resharding/warmup.

followUps: When would you use write-through or write-back instead of invalidate-on-write?
How do you prevent a synchronized mass-expiry from stampeding the DB?
How would a local in-process cache layer interact with the distributed cache?
How do you keep replicas consistent enough for warm failover?
How would you add persistence so a restart doesn't start fully cold?
